sessions.details: Recorded on 1979-10-22 for the John Peel Show, first broadcast on 29th October 1979.
Recorded on 1977-06-01 for the John Peel Show, first broadcast on 17th June 1977.

track_listing.details: excerpt; LP-Joe's Garage Acts II & III

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in Stadio on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Well hello there. Thomas the Vance here. TV on the radio. Welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
And this evening the first ever session by the London Heavy Band, a new bunch of people from
the East End called Iron Maiden. We have a recent session by The Damned which is full of humour
and from the archives three by UFO. We've got Classic Cuts by Hendrix, It's a Beautiful Day,
led zeppelin and van halen and new releases from anthony moore jackson brown bruce springsteen the
radio actors and frank zapper not forgetting our friday night connection that part of the
week that makes your brain go teased and tonight we start with molly hatchett impressive first
ever radio one session by iron maiden the track there was called iron maiden and they're currently
on tour around the united kingdom along with praying mantis praying mantis that is and they're
a good band more from them later prior to that you heard molly hatchett the track from their album
which is called Flirting With Disaster.
It came out in 79, which we're still in.
It was the title track.
A lot of people have written in, incidentally,
saying, would we please repeat the session
that we did with them at the Reading Festival?
Stay tuned till the new year.
And here's Jimi Hendrix.
One of the greatest bands in the world.
That's the first of four by The Damned.
The track was like Just Can't Be Happy.
Magnificent production put together by Tony Wilson
and the BBC Studios in October of this year.
And before that, on the Friday Rock Show,
something from an album which is called Flying Doesn't Help,
which is out on the Quango label by a gentleman called Anthony Moore.
The track was called War.
And if you haven't heard the name Anthony Moore before,
let me remind you that he has been in the bands
that were put together by Kevin Ayres and Henry Cow and Slap Happy.
Some of that really British ethnic rock.
Prior to that, of course, a real classic from way back in 1966,
Jimi Hendrix, The Late and Stone Free.
Especially for a gentleman by the name of Jebo
from a lady by the name of Mandy
with a message, tell him that I love him and I really miss him.
Jebo, that's the message.
Also, Hendrix was asked for by Guy Marshall of Failsworth.
I hope you enjoyed it, Guy.
A number of letters have been sort of coming in
after our Pink Floyd special,
saying, could we please help people to get tickets
when the Floyd come to town?
Well, to the best of our knowledge,
the Floyd are going to come to town
with their stage show of The Wall.
They're going to do five gigs at Wembley,
and that will be during the month of June in 1980.
As far as helping people get tickets,
well, I'd just love to be able to stand on a corner
and hand them out.
But as you know, I can't.
But I'm sure it will be well advertised in advance.
Here's another great classic.
Led Zeppelin, and thank you, from Led Zeppelin II, came out in 1969.
Especially for a gentleman whose name I don't know,
with lots and lots of love from his mother, whose name I do know,
Mrs. Arbola of No. 8 Belmont Road, Smethwick in Worley.
Whoever you are, sir, happy 21st birthday for last Monday.
Also, for a couple of lonely Led Zeppelin fans,
that's what they say here, that's for Lindsay,
who lives in Ayerscliff in Widowmead Drive in Presbury in Cheshire,
and also for Joanne of No. 7 Benbrook Way in Gasworth in Cheshire.
For the gentleman here, who had a birthday, I think, on December the 13th,
which, of course, was yesterday, and it seems that...
Oh, it's not a gentleman at all, it's a lady, a lady by the name of Debbie Lee.
And Debbie Lee and Nick and Dav and Steve and Jim and Jez
are having a gathering tonight.
Happy birthday for yesterday. I hope the gathering's good.
And also, Zeppelin was for a lady by the name of Pixie,
A little lady who probably is not really a pixie at all.
A lady called Sarah Lewis, with lots and lots of love from Dick Crabbe.
And before that, you heard a real classic from an album that was re-released last year by It's a Beautiful Day,
and it was called White Bird.
And now here's a warning to drivers on the A338 in Wiltshire.
A large tree has blown down and is across the road at Bowdenham, just south of Salisbury.
It's going to take about an hour to get the road clear again, so please avoid the area if you can.
That's the A338
It's blocked at Bodenham in Wiltshire
Because of a fallen tree
If you're driving, please do take care
And possibly it's a plane tree
Friday Rock Show
It's 23 and a half minutes now before 11 o'clock
One of these days I'll get the sink right on that traffic jingle
But nonetheless, to get me out of trouble
Here's Iron Maiden
And a track which is called Smash It Up
And before that you heard Iron Maiden and Running Free
There was a really big event in New York in September
between the 19th and the 23rd.
It was a whole series of people who bothered to hit the stage
at the Madison Square Garden, and they call themselves MUSE,
which I think stands for Musicians for the Use of Safe Energy.
Now, they've just brought out a triple album in this country
which is called No Nukes.
It features the talent of the Doobie Brothers, Jackson Brown,
Crosby, Stilton, Nash, James Taylor, and lots and lots of other people,
and it really is a superb album.
Inside the album, or the triple album, if you like,
there is a sort of log of events that relate to the accidents that have occurred
as far as nuclear energy creation has been developed on our planet,
and they're particularly worried about it.
It gives an extremely good and accurate, I think, rundown of the things that have gone wrong,
and essentially what these musicians are trying to say is that they hope that nothing goes wrong in the future
and maybe it would be a good idea if we sort of left it alone.
Anyway, here's a couple of tracks from the triple album, starting off with Jackson Brown.
This is called Before the Deluge.
Dolly Miss Molly, CeCe Ryder, Jenny Jenny and Jenny Take a Ride
by Bruce Springsteen and the E Street Band.
And before that, Jackson Browne and Before the Deluge.
Just two tracks which are available on this triple album
which has just come out on Asylum, which is called No Nukes.
I'm told by Tony Wilson and he's the man that knows about money.
This triple set is going to cost you something like £10
if you want to go out and buy it.
It's all the sort of result of this incredible series of concerts
that were put on at the Madison Square Garden in September
between the 19th and the 23rd
by the musicians for the use of safe energy.
Quite frightening, really,
to think that one day we may all be melted.
That was The Damned,
sending up I'm Free and Cars and Gangsters
and a track called I'm So Bored.
And before that, a single which is just out on the Charlie label.
It's by the Radio Actors and it's called Nuclear Waste.
And I wonder if you recognise the lead vocal.
Double track, though it was.
It was, in fact, Sting.
Two minutes after 11 on the Friday Rock Show here from BBC Radio 1,
and it's time to unveil the fact that John Harbridge,
who number five Croft Way, Croft Park, Menston, near Ilkley in West Yorkshire,
has copped himself a £10 record token.
He's the man that got it right.
Few arguments might ensue here, but for purposes best known to ourselves,
we consider the answer to be right.
Now, the tracks we played were Ride the Tiger by Jefferson Starship
from the album Dragonfly.
Then we played Anthem by Rush from Fly By Night.
And then from the album In Trance, it was The Scorpions and Top Of The Bill.
And the answer was insects.
Now, you might immediately say that a scorpion isn't an insect,
but if you were in the middle of the Sahara and you saw one scuttling across the sand toward you,
you probably would think it was.
But that's, as far as we're concerned, the right answer.
As I say, the man that got the £10 record token this week is John Harbridge
of near Ilkley, that's Menston, in West Yorkshire.
And John's put together his own Friday Night Connection,
three bits of music that have a theme.
Friday Night Connection put together by John Harbridge
at No. 5 Croft Way, Menston, near Ilkley in West Yorkshire.
Nice one, John.
If you think you know what the connection is
between those three pieces of music,
just jot your answer down on a postcard in a letter,
whatever form of communication you like,
and send it to the Friday Night Connection,
BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
Don't forget to put together your own Friday night connection as well.
Three bits of music with a theme or some sort of pattern.
You know what it's going to be.
We don't, but we trust somebody out there is going to suss you out.
And the one that we pull out of the bag, as we say here at the BBC,
will be the one that gets the £10 record token.
Just one thing.
Christmas is just around the corner, just in case you hadn't noticed.
Would you please get your cards, letters or whatever in just as soon as you can?
In fact, please post them tomorrow, too.
Friday Night Connection, BBC Radio 1, London W1A, 4WW.
And good luck.
It's 11.13, and now back to the 1st of the 6th of 1977
for the only three archive recordings we have of UFO.
But they're good, and here they are.
UFO, Phil Mogg on the vocals, Michael Shanker on the lead guitar,
Pete Wang on the bass guitar, Paul Raymond on keyboards and guitar,
and Andy Parker.
And that was the line-up on Wednesday 1st June 1977
when the band went into the BBC studios and laid those three tracks down.
The tracks they heard were too hot to handle.
Try Me and Lights Out.
And something I hate to tell you is that that really is all we have
in the archives of the BBC as far as UFO are concerned.
But now here's the good news.
We hope next week or the week after to have their new LP
and sometime during February, with a bit of luck,
we might be recording them in concert.
Great band currently on tour in the UK.
Now, it's 27 and a half minutes after 11 o'clock on the Friday Rock Show.
some time ago we played you a track from
Joe's Garage, or Garage, or Garage
whichever way you like to say it
by Frank Zappa, Act 1
now Acts 2 and 3 have just come out
and in that track that we played you
it was called Wet T-Shirt
well, it was the story of this nubile girl
by the name of Mary
she won the Wet T-Shirt contest
because she happened to be well endowed
you may be wondering what's happened to the lady
well, now you can find out
music is the best
wisdom, fanatic, corruption
of a short-gloved neck ornament
currently in resurgence.
And that's just an excerpt from a track called Packard Goose
from Joe's Garage Acts 2 and 3 by Frank Zappa.
We can only play you excerpts of the works of Frank Zappa,
the granddaddy of rock,
because about nine-tenths of everything that he puts down on the wax,
if you want to call it that, is completely unbroadcastable.
If you want to hear the naughty words, then you have to buy the album.
Right, now let's get back to Iron Maiden.
Got it live in Japan on the album Night After Night.
That's UK.
Whetton, Jobson and Bozio in the track called Caesar's Palace Blues.
They make an amazing noise for three people, they do.
That was specially for Geoffrey Smith of No. 15, Garth Road, Seven Oaks in Kent,
who really wanted to hear that track.
And before that you heard Iron Maiden and an instrumental entitled Transylvania.
They hope to have a single out in January and an album out in March.
Well, I'll be damned.
And a track from the 78 album called Van Halen.
The track was Running With The Devil.
Before that, Liar by The Dam.
That was our final track by The Dam tonight,
featuring the talents of Dave Manion, Ratscape is Captain Sensible and Al Ward,
who incidentally played the lead guitar on the track Liar.
Quick reminder to get your cards and letters in pretty darn quickly, if you would, please,
for the Friday Night Connection this week.
Christmas mail being what it is, it's rather difficult and it tends to get clogged up.
Send your answers to Friday Night Connection, BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
Nine and a half minutes to midnight, the last by Iron Maiden.
...from the album Live Rust and the Loner.
Before that, Iron Maiden and Sanctuary.
Beautiful, beautiful session by Iron Maiden.
Going to be big, I think, in the 80s.
Dedicate that last track to the Randolph Avenue and Randolph Crescent Maida Vale London Dragons,
two of whom are in the studio, and I'm married to one of them.
On rock on tomorrow at four o'clock on Radio 1, Phil Lynott,
and a track from his new solo LP, which is called Solo in Soho.
Also, The Clash, talking about London Calling, their monstrously successful new album.
In concert tomorrow is Two Tone Totally, the specials and the selector
Next week on the Friday Rock Show, the Moody Blues from the Archives
And a new band called Terranova
Our secretary is Sue and our engineer was Paul, thank you Paul
Our producer is Tony Wilson who will now speak
Say goodnight Tommy
We're going to rock on there sir
See you tomorrow, God bless
